title: Geodesics and Laplace spectrum on 3D contact sub-Riemannian manifolds: the Reeb flow
speaker: Yves Colin de Verdière
abstract: Joint work with Luc Hillairet (Orléans) and Emmanuel Trélat (Paris). A 3D closed manifold with a contact distribution and a metric on it carries a canonical contact form. The associated Reeb flow plays a central role for the asymptotics of the geodesics and for the spectral asymptotics of the Laplace operator. I plan to describe it using some Birkhoff normal forms.

proof_stages.label: Contact sub-Riemannian and Reeb data
proof_stages.summary: The talk fixes a 3-dimensional contact distribution, defines contact forms, the associated Reeb vector field and its Hamiltonian lift on the characteristic cone, then adds a sub-Riemannian metric whose canonical contact form makes the Reeb flow the limiting dynamics at the boundary of the cosphere compactification.

proof_stages.label: Flat Heisenberg normal model
proof_stages.summary: The compact Heisenberg quotient is used as the solvable flat model, with explicit horizontal frame, contact form, Reeb field, Laplacian spectrum, and Hamiltonian factorization into a Reeb Hamiltonian times a harmonic oscillator, showing geodesics spiralling around Reeb orbits.

proof_stages.label: Birkhoff normal form near the characteristic cone
proof_stages.summary: The Heisenberg decoupling is extended microlocally by a homogeneous canonical transformation near Sigma, expressing the dual metric as a formal Reeb-harmonic-oscillator expansion with flat remainder and giving long-time approximation of geodesics by slow Reeb motion plus fast transverse rotation.

proof_stages.label: Closed geodesics accumulating on Reeb orbits
proof_stages.summary: Assuming convergence of the normal form near a closed Reeb orbit yields families of closed geodesics spiralling around it, motivating the conjecture that every nondegenerate periodic Reeb orbit is the topological limit of infinitely many closed geodesics.

proof_stages.label: Sub-Riemannian Laplacian, Weyl law, and quantum limits
proof_stages.summary: After defining the self-adjoint subelliptic Laplacian, the talk states the Weyl law with contact-volume coefficient and describes quantum limits: most high-energy eigenfunctions concentrate microlocally on the characteristic cone and have Reeb-invariant limits, with quantum ergodicity when the Reeb flow is ergodic.

proof_stages.label: Toeplitz quantization and quantum Birkhoff normal form
proof_stages.summary: Using the Boutet de Monvel-Guillemin Toeplitz calculus on the symplectic cone Sigma, the Reeb Hamiltonian and Birkhoff normal form are quantized so that the Laplacian near Sigma decomposes into harmonic-oscillator levels with effective Toeplitz operators Delta_l whose spectra approximate corresponding pieces of the Laplace spectrum.

proof_stages.label: Trace formulas and the spectral Reeb-period conjecture
proof_stages.summary: The final argument reviews trace formulas, compares the Riemannian, Heisenberg, Melrose, and Toeplitz cases, observes how closed-geodesic lengths and Reeb periods appear in the model formulas, and formulates the conjecture that Reeb periods are spectral invariants of the 3D contact sub-Riemannian Laplacian.

examples.name: Magnetic principal circle bundle over a surface
examples.note: A principal S^1-bundle with connection is used to make the horizontal distribution into a contact sub-Riemannian structure, illustrating how sub-Riemannian geodesics project to magnetic trajectories and how Reeb orbits follow level lines of the magnetic scalar.
examples.engagement_reason: The magnetic circle-bundle example is set up with connection, curvature/magnetic scalar, momentum, projected magnetic trajectories, and an explicit Reeb-field description.

passes.reason: A principal circle bundle with connection over a surface is a standard magnetic contact sub-Riemannian family used illustratively.

examples.name: Unit cotangent bundle of a Riemannian surface
examples.note: Taking M=S^*X with its tautological/contact form gives a contact sub-Riemannian example whose Reeb vector field is the Riemannian geodesic flow, making the later Reeb-ergodicity statement comparable to ordinary quantum ergodicity.
examples.engagement_reason: The unit cotangent bundle example is introduced and its contact form/Reeb flow are identified, but the speaker does not derive or compute anything on it.

passes.reason: The unit cotangent bundle with its canonical contact structure is a standard construction relating Reeb and geodesic flows.
The unit cotangent bundle with its canonical contact structure is a natural representative construction for any Riemannian surface.
The unit cotangent bundle construction is the ordinary contact model whose Reeb flow is the geodesic flow, with no special extremal or exceptional role here.
a standard unit-cotangent-bundle construction whose Reeb flow is the geodesic flow, not a sporadic or limiting case

examples.name: Arnold asymptotic linking invariant on S^3
examples.note: For M=S^3 the Weyl-law contact-volume coefficient is identified with Arnold's asymptotic linking number for long Reeb orbits, illustrating that the spectrum already contains a dynamical invariant of the Reeb flow.
examples.engagement_reason: Arnold’s asymptotic linking invariant on S^3 is cited as an interpretation of the Weyl-volume integral and briefly described, not worked out.

examples.name: Poisson trace formula for the flat torus
examples.note: The Poisson summation formula is presented as the elementary spectral trace formula in which Laplace eigenvalues on a flat torus are dual to lengths of closed geodesics, serving as a comparison for the desired sub-Riemannian trace formula.
examples.engagement_reason: The Poisson formula for the flat torus is written explicitly and interpreted term-by-term as eigenvalues versus closed-geodesic lengths in a trace formula.

passes.reason: The Poisson trace formula is presented for the homogeneous flat torus/lattice model, a highly regular symmetric setting.
The flat torus is the highly regular translation-invariant model where Poisson summation gives the prototype exact trace formula.

examples.name: Embedded contact homology capacities recovering contact volume
examples.note: In response to a question, ECH capacities and periodic Reeb orbits are mentioned as another mechanism by which Reeb dynamics can recover the contact volume, suggesting but not supplying a route toward the spectral Reeb-period conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: Embedded contact homology capacities are brought up in discussion as a known minimax construction whose limit recovers contact volume, without a concrete computation or derivation.

passes.reason: ECH capacities are min–max action thresholds whose asymptotics recover contact volume, used as supporting structure rather than a refutation.
Embedded contact homology capacities form a systematic sequence of contact/symplectic invariants used to recover volume from Reeb dynamics.
ECH capacities are action-filtered min-max spectral invariants whose asymptotic limit recovers contact volume, so their structure is extremal rather than adversarial.
